revision.comment: Bot: remove [[Category:Sn crud]] (crud category cleanup) [[github:EmmaLeonhart/shintowiki-scripts/actions/runs/23984597483|manual triggered pipeline]]
revision.text:
[[ja:月江寺]] {{Infobox Buddhist temple|image=File:Gekkohji Temple01.JPG|religious_affiliation={{ill|Rinzai school|qid=Q639625|1=Rinzai school}}|prefecture={{ill|Fujiyoshida|qid=Q647508|1=Fujiyoshida}}|coordinates={{Coord|35.493389|N|138.800028|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline,title}}|deity={{ill|Kṣitigarbha|qid=Q604687|1=Kṣitigarbha}}|part_of={{ill|Q6581638|qid=Q6581638|1=Q6581638}}}} {{Temple in Japan | Name = {{ruby|Gekkō-ji|げっこうじ}} | image = [[File:Gekkohji Temple01.JPG|270px]]
Honden (Main Hall) (August 2010) | Location = Fujiyoshida City, Yamanashi Prefecture | Coordinates = {{Coord|35|29|36.2|N|138|48|00.1|E|scale:20000_region:JP-19_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} | Mountain = Suijōzan (Water Above Mountain) | Sect = Rinzai Zen Myōshinji Sect | Principal image = Jizō Bosatsu (Kṣitigarbha) | Established = | Founder = | Re-established = | Re-founder = | Formal Name = | Alternate Names = | Pilgrimage = [[Kai 108 Sacred Sites]] No. 29 | Cultural Properties = | Map = {{Location map|Japan Yamanashi Prefecture|label=Gekkō-ji|float=center|relief=1|width=256}} }} '''Gekkō-ji''' (月江寺) is a [[:en:Rinzai Zen|Rinzai Zen]] Buddhist temple located in [[Shimo-Yoshida]], [[Fujiyoshida City]], {{ill|Yamanashi Prefecture|en|Yamanashi Prefecture|qid=Q132720|lt=Yamanashi Prefecture|1=Yamanashi Prefecture}}. It belongs to the [[:en:Myōshinji|Myōshinji]] branch of the Rinzai Zen sect and is named after its mountain, Suijōzan (水上山). Its principal image is Jizō Bosatsu (Kṣitigarbha). == History == Located in Shimo-Yoshida, Fujiyoshida City, Gekkō-ji stands on the northwestern bank of the [[Miyagawa River (Yamanashi)|Miyagawa River]]. Alongside the [[Jishū]] temple [[Sainen-ji (Fujiyoshida City)|Sainen-ji]], it was one of the prominent temples in the region, holding significant [[shuinchi|land grants]]. According to the ''[[Kai Kokushi]]'', the temple was initially a [[Tendai sect]] temple named Shōkō-in, serving as a prayer site for [[Shimo-miya Sengen Shrine|Shimo-Miya Sengen Shrine]]. After falling into decline, it was restored by [[Zetsugaku Sonei]], a disciple of [[Bakutai Tokushō]], the founder of {{ill|Kōgaku-ji|en|Kōgaku-ji|qid=Q3198275|lt=Kōgaku-ji|1=Kōgaku-ji}} in Enzan, during the medieval period of the {{ill|Kai Province|en|Kai Province|qid=Q858076|lt=Kai Province|1=Kai Province}}. The restored temple, renamed Gekkō-an and affiliated with Kōgaku-ji, became a base for Rinzai Zen in the Fuji area. The ''Kai Kokushi'' attributes the restoration to 1395 (Ōei 2), and the ''Katsuyama-ki'' records Gekkō-ji's head monk serving a rotation at Kōgaku-ji in 1529 (Kyōroku 2). While documents confirming land donations are lacking, it is known that Kato Mitsuyoshi of [[Kato clan]] donated land in 1591 (Tensho 19). Other records indicate a donation of 8 ''koku'' of rice fields by a descendant of Kobayashi Owari-no-kami, and in 1649 (Keian 2), {{ill|Tokugawa Iemitsu|en|Tokugawa Iemitsu|qid=Q314481|lt=Tokugawa Iemitsu|1=Tokugawa Iemitsu}} granted 16 ''koku'' of temple land through a [[shuinjō]] (red-seal document). During the {{ill|Edo period|en|Edo period|qid=Q184963|lt=Edo period|1=Edo period}}, Gekkō-ji relocated to its current site in Shimo-Yoshida. In the Kan'ei era, Zenjin Shōetsu from Edo's Kaizen-ji temple assumed leadership, aligning the temple with the [[:en:Myōshinji|Myōshinji]] sect of Rinzai Zen. Gekkō-ji established a network of 20 branch temples and formed a [[Monzen-machi|Monzen-machi]] (temple town), becoming the largest temple in the Fuji area. Fires destroyed the temple complex in 1726 (Kyōhō 11), the Bunka era, and 1836 (Tenpō 8). Rebuilding efforts during the late Edo period included the creation of many Buddhist statues. During the mid-Edo period, disputes with [[Shimo-Miya Sengen Shrine|Shimo-Miya Sengen Shrine]] regarding its status as the guardian shrine escalated, with tensions peaking during the Hōei (1706) and Shōtoku eras. These disputes continued into the Meiji period, resulting in the removal of Gekkō-ji monks and Buddhist artifacts during the anti-Buddhist movement [[Haibutsu Kishaku]]. At [[Kitaguchi Hongū Fuji Sengen Shrine]], where Gekkō-ji had served as the temple, the destruction of the Nio-mon gate and bell tower further illustrates this turbulent period. Today, the site is part of Gekkō-ji Park. In 1957 (Shōwa 32), Gekkō-ji's chief priest Yamada Shūhō founded the Gekkō-ji Gakuen educational institution, which includes Gekkō-ji Kindergarten and [[Fuji Gakuen Junior High and High School]]. File:Gekkohji Temple ike.jpg|Gekkō-ji Park, featuring the pond that inspired the temple's name, Suijōzan ("Water Above Mountain"). Previously connected to [[Shimo-Miya Sengen Shrine]] by a sacred forest. File:Old kakuta clinic.jpg|Old Kakuta Clinic, built in 1928 as a high-end restaurant. Constructed by Imperial Household craftsman Izumi Tarō, it reflects the prosperity of Nishigura, developed from Gekkō-ji's temple town. File:FujiGakuen.jpg|Fuji Gakuen High School, now occupying much of the temple grounds. == Gekkō-ji and Fuji Worship == Gekkō-ji is closely associated with [[Fuji worship]]. In the Sengoku period, the area featured a purification site for Fuji pilgrims and hosted a checkpoint. Pilgrims began their ascents here, receiving talismans and blessings. During the Edo period, the rise of [[Fuji-kō]] saw Gekkō-ji's prominence as the first pilgrimage station diminish. However, the temple maintained significant influence, providing talismans and prayers at key points along the Yoshida Trail. It also served as a [[bettō-ji]] (temple) for [[Kitaguchi Hongū Fuji Sengen Shrine]]. File:Gekkōji Temple Aizen.jpg|The Aizen Jizō-dō, a detached part of Gekkō-ji. Fuji pilgrims once purified themselves here using spring water, and a Sengoku-era checkpoint was located nearby. File:Gekkōji Temple Aizen and FujisanSimomiya Omurosengen KotohiraShrine.jpg|The Aizen Jizō-dō alongside the [[Fujisan Shitamiya Komuro Sengen Shrine|Shitamiya Kotohira Shrine]], showcasing the connection between the two. File:Fuji Sekisyo .jpg|A stone monument marking the site of Fuji's first checkpoint, alongside other commemorative markers. == Treasures == Gekkō-ji holds numerous artifacts, including portraits of its founder Zetsugaku Sonei, Koku-shi, and other distinguished Zen masters. Paintings donated by the Akimoto clan of [[Tanimura Domain]] are also preserved. == References == * Akira Akiyama, "The Historical Background of the Gekkō-ji Records," ''Fujiyoshida Historical Studies No. 11,'' Fujiyoshida City Board of Education, 1996. * Akira Akiyama, "The Development of Fuji Worship and Culture," ''Fujiyoshida History,'' Medieval Volume 2, Chapter 3, 1999. * Kenchō Inukai, "Trends of Regional Temples in the Mid-Edo Period," ''Fujiyoshida History.'' * ''580th Memorial of the Founding of Gekkō-ji: The Zen Art of Fuji Northern Slopes,'' Fujiyoshida Historical Folklore Museum, 2009. == See Also == * {{ill|Gekkōji Station|en|Gekkōji Station|qid=Q5530322|lt=Gekkōji Station|1=Gekkōji Station}} - A station on the {{ill|Fujikyuko Line|en|Fujikyuko Line|qid=Q5507433|lt=Fujikyuko Line|1=Fujikyuko Line}} operated by [[Fujikyu Railway]]. {{translated page|ja|月江寺|version=95318122|comment=Imported full ja history}} {{Commons category|Gekkō-ji (Fujiyoshida)}} [[ja:月江寺]] {{wikidata link|Q11516163}} [[Category:Buddhist temples in Yamanashi Prefecture]] [[Category:History of Fujiyoshida City]] [[Category:Kai 108 Pilgrimage Sites]] [[Category:Myoshin-ji temples]] [[Category:Wikidata has short description]]
